intro: Yes, the jokes about British food are outdated—spectacularly so. London now out-eats almost any city on earth: more three-Michelin-star kitchens than ever, the best Indian food outside the subcontinent, a craft beer scene born under railway arches, and pie-and-mash shops that have survived since Victoria was on the throne. The gastropub was invented here, chicken tikka masala was (probably) invented here, and the humble chippy soldiers on with dignity. Here's how to eat and drink properly.

sections.title: The Essentials
sections.content:
Start your morning with a **Full English**—the complete works of beans, bacon, sausage, eggs, mushrooms, tomato, toast, and black pudding (if you're brave). Caffs (greasy spoons) serve it best; anywhere with formica tables and builders reading tabloids is a good sign. Coffee culture has arrived—London's specialty scene (Monmouth, Kaffeine, Prufrock) rivals Melbourne—but a builder's tea (strong, milk, no pretension) remains the backbone of civilization.

**Lunch** is often grabbed quickly—Pret and supermarket meal deals fuel the city—but proper lunch exists at pubs serving gastro fare, at Padella's pasta counter, or grazing Borough Market's extraordinary stalls. Sunday lunch, however, is **sacred**: a roast with all the trimmings, Yorkshire pudding mandatory, gravy essential. Blacklock does the best-value version in the centre; good gastropubs book out a week ahead.

**Dinner** starts earlier than Paris (7pm is normal) and kitchens close earlier too—pubs often stop serving food by 9pm. Booking is essential for popular restaurants; OpenTable, Resy and SevenRooms all operate here. Walk-ins work at market stalls, counter spots (Barrafina, Kiln), curry houses, and Chinatown.

**Budget guide:** a market lunch runs £8-12, a curry feast at Tayyabs £20-25 a head, mid-range dinner with drinks £40-60, starred tasting menus £150-300+. Pints are £6-7.50 in Zone 1; cocktails £12-25 depending on postcode.

sections.title: What to Eat
sections.content:
**Full English Breakfast** — The complete works. Variations exist (Scottish adds haggis, Welsh adds laverbread), but the principle is sacred: protein, carbs, and enough to fuel a morning. E. Pellicci in Bethnal Green (1900, family-run, Grade II-listed marquetry interior) is the platonic caff.

**Pie & Mash** — London's original fast food. Minced beef pie with mash and liquor (parsley sauce, not alcohol—a common confusion), eaten with a spoon and fork. M. Manze on Tower Bridge Road has served it since 1902 for the price of a Mayfair coffee. Jellied eels are the historic add-on; try them once, for the story.

**Fish & Chips** — Vinegar application is a personal matter, but it should be applied. Mushy peas are traditional. The Golden Hind in Marylebone has fried since 1914; Poppies in Spitalfields adds 1950s jukebox nostalgia.

**Sunday Roast** — Beef, lamb, chicken, or pork with roast potatoes, Yorkshire pudding, and gravy. A 2-3 hour social affair, not a quick meal. Blacklock's bottomless-gravy version is legendary; gastropubs across the city compete fiercely.

**Curry** — Britain's adopted national dish, and London does it at every altitude: sizzling lamb chops and karahi at Tayyabs or Lahore Kebab House in Whitechapel (BYOB, £25 a head), Bombay-café comfort at Dishoom, and two-Michelin-starred game biryani at Gymkhana in Mayfair. Brick Lane is famous but inconsistent—go for the bagels and the history, eat your curry in Whitechapel.

**Salt Beef Beigel** — From Beigel Bake on Brick Lane, open 24 hours since 1974: hot hand-carved salt beef, ferocious mustard, a pickle, about £7. One of the world's great sandwiches at 2am.

**Afternoon Tea** — Scones (cream first in Devon, jam first in Cornwall—don't ask), finger sandwiches, cakes, and proper tea. Splurge tier: Claridge's or Fortnum & Mason's Diamond Jubilee Tea Salon (£80-110, book weeks ahead, dress up). Affordable tier: a cream tea—just scones, jam, clotted cream and a pot—at any good bakery delivers most of the joy for under £15.

**Modern British** — The nose-to-tail revolution started at St. John in 1994 (bone marrow, Eccles cakes, zero apology) and its descendants—Brat's wood-grilled turbot, Kiln's clay pots—now define the city's cooking.

sections.title: Where to Drink
sections.content:
**The Pub** is not a bar—it's an institution. Find "your local" and become a regular. Order at the bar (not the table), pay immediately, tip by rounding up or saying "and one for yourself." The bell means last orders—get to the bar. For history with your pint: Ye Olde Cheshire Cheese (rebuilt 1667, Dickens's haunt, gloriously gloomy), The George Inn (London's last galleried coaching inn, 1670s), and The Blackfriar (1905 Arts and Crafts fever dream).

**Real Ale** is the traditional choice—cask-conditioned, served at cellar temperature, with names like "Old Peculier" and "Bishop's Finger." Craft beer arrived in 2009 when The Kernel started brewing under a Bermondsey railway arch and spawned the **Bermondsey Beer Mile**: half a dozen taprooms strung along the arches, open mostly Friday-Sunday. Do it on a Saturday with Maltby Street Market as your feeding station.

**Wine Bars** have improved dramatically. Gordon's (1890, candlelit cellar off Villiers Street) is the atmospheric ancient; Noble Rot on Lamb's Conduit Street pairs one of Britain's great lists with slip-soles in smoked butter; 40 Maltby Street pours natural wine in a working Bermondsey warehouse; Hackney and Peckham are colonized by low-intervention spots.

**Cocktails** run from sacred to cutting-edge. The American Bar at the Savoy has poured since the 1890s—order the Hanky Panky where it was invented. The Connaught Bar's tableside martini trolley has topped the World's 50 Best list. At the other pole: Tayēr + Elementary on Old Street (the bartenders' favourite bar) and Satan's Whiskers in Bethnal Green, where the classics-only menu changes daily. Expect £12-15 out east, £20-25 in Mayfair.

**Gin** deserves mention. London Dry is a style, and the city takes it seriously—from historic gin palaces to distillery bars. A G&T is always an acceptable order.

sections.title: Neighborhoods for Food
sections.content:
**Borough/Bermondsey (SE1)** — London's greatest food quarter: Borough Market (trading since 1014—go hungry, go weekday mornings), Padella's pasta, M. Manze's pie and mash, Maltby Street Market, 40 Maltby Street's natural wine, and the Beer Mile. You could eat here for a week.

**Brick Lane/Whitechapel (E1)** — The immigration story in food: 24-hour Jewish bagel bakeries, the Bangladeshi curry mile, and—crucially—Tayyabs and Lahore Kebab House ten minutes south for the Punjabi grills locals actually queue for.

**Soho & Chinatown (W1)** — The densest eating in Britain: Kiln's clay pots, Barrafina's counter, Hoppers' Sri Lankan pancakes, Gerrard Street's roast duck windows (Four Seasons is the classic), plus the city's best cocktail and coffee density.

**Shoreditch/Hackney (E2/E8)** — The young, creative scene: Brat's wood-fire cooking above a pub, Vietnamese on Kingsland Road, Broadway Market Saturdays, natural wine bars, and Satan's Whiskers nearby in Bethnal Green.

**Mayfair (W1)** — Where the stars cluster: Sketch's three-starred Lecture Room, Gymkhana's two-starred Indian, the Connaught Bar. Set-lunch menus make these rooms accessible at a third of dinner prices.

**Brixton (SW2)** — Caribbean heritage in the Brixton Village arcades, the original Franco Manca, jerk chicken and rum bars—some of London's best-value eating.

**Notting Hill (W11)** — Portobello Road Market's Caribbean and Spanish stalls plus, remarkably, two of Britain's few three-star restaurants (The Ledbury, Core by Clare Smyth) within walking distance of each other.

sections.title: Practical Tips
sections.content:
**Reservations** — Essential for dinner at popular spots; the hottest tables (Core, The Ledbury, Ikoyi) release months ahead and vanish in hours. Counter restaurants (Barrafina, Kiln, Padella) hold seats for walk-ins—arrive at opening or expect a wait, usually with a virtual queue app.

**Tipping** — 12.5% service is usually added automatically; check the bill and don't double-tip. If not included, 10-12.5% is standard. Pubs: not expected, but "and one for yourself" is a lovely tradition.

**Water** — Free tap water is a legal right in licensed premises. Just ask.

**BYOB** — A glorious money-saver: Tayyabs, The Golden Hind and many curry houses let you bring your own bottle for little or no corkage. There's usually an off-licence within a hundred yards; that's not a coincidence.

**Sunday** — Many restaurants close or serve only roast; roasts book out. Markets compensate: Columbia Road flowers, Brick Lane, and Borough (now open Sundays too). Pubs are your friend.

**Timing quirks** — Pie and mash shops close mid-afternoon. Beer Mile taprooms open mostly Friday-Sunday. Beigel Bake never closes at all. Pub kitchens stop around 9pm even when the bar pours until 11.

**August** — Unlike Paris, London doesn't close. Restaurants operate normally.

highlights.name: Afternoon Tea (Splurge or Cream-Tea Cheap)
highlights.neighborhood: Mayfair/Piccadilly (Claridge's, Fortnum's) or any good bakery

highlights.name: Pub Session at a Historic Boozer
highlights.neighborhood: Fleet Street (Ye Olde Cheshire Cheese) or Southwark (The George Inn)
